title: Bloomberg: Trump Will Discuss Leaving NATO in Meeting With Rutte — White House Confirms Withdrawal Is 'On the Table'
detail: Bloomberg reported on April 7 that President Trump plans to raise the prospect of US withdrawal from NATO in his upcoming meeting with Secretary General Mark Rutte scheduled for April 8. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed the reporting, telling reporters that NATO withdrawal is 'something the president has discussed, and I think it's something the president will be discussing in a couple of hours with Secretary-General Rutte.' Leavitt framed allies' refusal to participate in the Iran war as a fundamental test of the alliance that European members had failed: 'They turned their backs on us.' The announcement that Trump intended to formally raise withdrawal in the closed-door session marked the clearest indication yet that US departure from the alliance was now being considered as a live policy option rather than a rhetorical pressure tactic. Stars and Stripes described 'NATO fate looming over Rutte's White House sit-down.' The Notus outlet reported Rutte had arrived in Washington on an alliance 'rescue mission.'

media.caption: Bloomberg: Trump Will Discuss Leaving NATO When He Meets Alliance Chief

title: Rutte Arrives in Washington; Meets Rubio at State Dept Ahead of White House Meeting
detail: NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte arrived in Washington on April 7-8 and met separately with Secretary of State Marco Rubio at the State Department ahead of his closed-door White House session with Trump. Rubio — who had recently stated the US would 're-examine the value of NATO' after the Iran war — received Rutte's pre-meeting briefing in a session described by officials as 'frank.' The Rutte-Rubio meeting at State was seen as preliminary groundwork for the more critical Trump encounter. Outlets including Notus described Rutte's Washington trip as an alliance 'rescue mission' — an attempt by the NATO Secretary General to convince Trump that the alliance still served US interests and head off any formal withdrawal announcement. Rutte's approach — described as 'deferential' and 'Trump whisperer' diplomacy — was under increasing scrutiny from European governments who believed a firmer stance was needed.
